elements.sent-2411-001: In the theory with a Majorana fermion ($\Chi$) coupled to quark-like fermions ($Q$) via a dimension-six four-fermion vector-vector interaction, we have computed in an earlier work the baryon asymmetry generated in the decay and scattering processes of the $\Chi$ with $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work we consider such processes in the expanding early Universe, set up the Boltzmann equations governing the $\Chi$ and net baryon number densities, and numerically solve them in example benchmark points, taking the thermally averaged decay and scattering rates and their temperature dependence from the earlier study.
elements.sent-2411-003: We find that starting from a baryon symmetric Universe at early time, the presently observed baryon asymmetry of the Universe (BAU) can be explained in this theory over a wide range of mass scales, $M_\chi\in (10^4,10^{16 )$~GeV for appropriately chosen couplings.}
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that scattering processes play a crucial role in generating the baryon asymmetry in this theory.
elements.sent-2411-005: We present our results in a general manner that should be useful not just in our theory, but also in other related theories that share the essential ingredients.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results should help guide promising ways to probe such new physics in terrestrial experiments.
elements.sent-2411-007: Observationally, it is apparent that we live in a Universe in which there is more matter than antimatter at the present epoch, the so-called baryon asymmetry of the Universe (BAU).
elements.sent-2411-008: If the Big-Bang produced matter and antimatter in equal amounts, from a particle physics perspective, how at late time the BAU emerges remains an open question.
elements.sent-2411-009: For the asymmetry to develop, the three Sakharov conditions~\cite{Sakharov:1967dj must be satisfied in any candidate theory, namely, $C$ and $CP$ violation, baryon number $B$ violation, and departure from thermal equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Although the standard model (SM) of particle physics has these necessary ingredients for producing an asymmetry, it is commonly believed that it falls short in explaining the observed BAU by many orders of magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-011: Various theories beyond the standard model (BSM) have been put forth to explain the observed BAU~(for reviews, see for example Refs.~\cite{Kolb:1990vq,Cline:2006ts ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: In Ref.~\cite{Gopalakrishna:2022hwk we put forward one such proposal containing a Majorana fermion pair with indefinite baryon number coupled to quark-like fermions, and showed that the three Sakharov conditions can be satisfied in the theory, enabling the generation of the BAU.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The theory extends the SM with a new electromagnetic (EM) charge neutral Dirac fermion $\chi$ which couples to an up-type color-triplet quark-like fermion $\Up$ with EM charge $+2/3$, and to two color-triplet down-type quark-like fermions $\Dp$ with EM charge $-1/3$, via a dimension-six four-fermion vector-vector (VV) effective operator $(1/2\Lambda^2) [\overline{\Dp^c_b \, \gamma^\mu \Dp_a ] \ [ \bar{\chi \, \gamma_\mu \left( g_L P_L + g_R P_R \right) \Up_c ] \, \epsilon^{abc}$, where $\Lambda$ is the cut-off scale, the subscripts $a,b,c$ on the fields are the color indices, and $D^c$ is the charge conjugate field.}}
